title: Arrhythmia — Ventricular Fibrillation
oneLiner: Chaotic, pulseless ventricular rhythm causing cardiac arrest; immediate defibrillation is the only effective treatment—time is brain and heart.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Pulseless Ventricular Tachycardia (VT):** Treat identically to VF—shock immediately. Single finding: ECG showing regular broad-complex tachycardia with loss of cardiac output; defibrillator perceives a shockable rhythm.
- **Asystole / PEA:** Shock may waste time. Single finding: defibrillator shows a non-shockable rhythm (flatline or organised complexes but no pulse) → switch to CPR, adrenaline, and reversible cause search immediately.
- **Tension pneumothorax causing PEA:** Look for tracheal deviation, absent breath sounds, distended neck veins, hypotension. Immediate needle decompression before any other intervention.
sections.Presentation:
- Sudden collapse, unresponsive, no normal breathing (agonal gasps may occur in first minutes)
- No palpable carotid or femoral pulse
- Seizure-like activity (global cerebral hypoxia) can be confused with epilepsy
- Skin colour: pale, grey, then cyanotic
- ECG / defibrillator monitor: chaotic, irregular ventricular waveform, no identifiable QRS complexes
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Most likely:** Pulseless VT → same rhythm on monitor but wider, more organised complexes; treat with immediate defibrillation. **Splitter:** Defibrillator rhythm check: shockable vs. non-shockable. VF/VT require defibrillation; asystole/PEA do not.
- **Less likely:** Asystole (flatline, no electrical activity); PEA (organised rhythm but no output); fine VF may mimic asystole on ECG – check gain and look for any electrical activity.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside (first hour):**  
  – Attach defibrillator pads immediately for rhythm analysis (do not delay defibrillation)  
  – Check pulse and breathing before rhythm check? No – in witnessed arrest with monitor showing shockable rhythm, defibrillate first.  
  – After ROSC: 12-lead ECG, point-of-care ultrasound (if skilled) for LV function, tamponade, pneumothorax.
- **Bloods (after ROSC):**  
  – Arterial blood gas: pH, pCO2, base deficit, lactate, glucose, K+, Ca2+  
  – FBC, U&E, Mg2+, troponin (high-sensitivity), coagulation screen, D-dimer (if PE suspected)  
  – Consider toxicology screen if suspected overdose.
- **Imaging (after ROSC, stable):**  
  – CXR: endotracheal tube placement, tension pneumothorax, aspiration  
  – CT head: if suspected intracranial cause  
  – CT pulmonary angiography: if PE suspected (high D-dimer, low PaO2/FiO2)
- **Special tests:**  
  – Echocardiogram within 24h to assess LV function and structural cause  
  – Coronary angiography urgently if ST-elevation on post-ROSC ECG (or high suspicion of ACS)
- **Never-miss test:**  
  – **Immediate rhythm check via defibrillator pads** – do not waste time placing leads; treat based on defibrillator rhythm. Do not delay defibrillation for any other test.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**  
  – Call cardiac arrest team (2222) – or pull emergency bell.  
  – Start high-quality CPR immediately (30:2) while defibrillator is prepared.  
  – Defibrillate: biphasic 150–200 J (monophasic 360 J). After shock, resume CPR immediately for 2 minutes without checking pulse/rhythm.  
  – If still in VF/VT: defibrillate again, continue CPR cycles.  
  – After 3 shocks: give adrenaline 1mg IV and amiodarone 300mg IV after the 3rd shock (then 150mg after 5th shock if refractory).  
  – Follow Resuscitation Council UK ALS algorithm: shockable rhythm pathway.  
  – Treat reversible causes: 4Hs + 4Ts (hypoxia, hypovolaemia, hypo-/hyperkalaemia, hypothermia; thrombosis [MI/PE], tension pneumothorax, tamponade, toxins).
- **Ward (first 24–72h) – if ROSC achieved:**  
  – Targeted temperature management (32–36°C) for 24h if unconscious – use cooling blanket, ice packs, cold IV saline (check local protocol).  
  – Transfer to ICU/cardiac high-dependency for post-cardiac arrest syndrome management.  
  – Urgent echocardiogram and cardiology review for ICD consideration.  
  – Treat underlying cause: if STEMI → primary PCI; if electrolyte disturbance → correct (K+ 4.5–5.0, Mg2+ 0.8–1.2 mmol/L).  
  – Neurological assessment: pupillary response, corneal reflexes, motor responses. Discuss neuroprognostication with senior.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**  
  – ICD implantation for all survivors of VF not due to reversible cause.  
  – Genetic counselling if channelopathy suspected (e.g., Brugada, long QT).  
  – Cardiology follow-up at 6–8 weeks, then annually.  
  – Consider cardiac MRI if myocarditis or structural disease possible.
- **Default orders (if admitted post-ROSC):**  
  – Fluids: balanced crystalloid (e.g., Plasmalyte) 30 mL/kg loading if hypotensive; maintenance at 1 mL/kg/h (adjust for renal function)  
  – VTE prophylaxis: subcutaneous low molecular weight heparin when no active bleeding (e.g., enoxaparin 40mg daily, but hold for 24h if thrombolysis given)  
  – Diet: NBM until safe swallow assessed (GCS <12 → keep NBM for airway protection)  
  – Observations: continuous ECG monitoring, hourly neuro status (GCS, pupil size), BP, SpO2, capnography if intubated. Level 2 care (HDU) minimum.
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents:**  
  – Adrenaline 1mg (1:10,000) IV every 3–5 minutes during cardiac arrest.  
  – Amiodarone 300mg IV (over 5 minutes) after 3rd shock for refractory VF/VT; second dose 150mg IV after 5th shock.  
- **Common alternatives:**  
  – Lidocaine 1–1.5 mg/kg IV (only if amiodarone unavailable or contraindicated) – not first line.  
  – Magnesium sulfate 2g IV (over 1–2 minutes) for polymorphic VT/torsade de pointes (especially if low Mg2+).  
  – Sodium bicarbonate 50 mmol IV only if severe acidosis (pH <7.1) or tricyclic overdose – [check local policy].  
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**  
  – Amiodarone: caution with other QTc-prolonging drugs (e.g., macrolides, ciprofloxacin, antipsychotics).  
  – Adrenaline: may increase myocardial O2 demand; avoid in hypothermic arrest until core >30°C.  
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**  
  – No major dose adjustment for amiodarone or adrenaline in renal impairment.  
  – Amiodarone hepatic excretion – avoid in severe liver disease if possible, or reduce dose (discuss with pharmacist).  
- **Most dangerous dose error:**  
  – **Do not give IV amiodarone as a push in a conscious patient (risk of hypotension/bradycardia).** In cardiac arrest, push slowly over 5 minutes only if directed by ALS algorithm.  
  – **Adrenaline: NEVER give as rapid IV bolus in a patient with pulse – risk of malignant ventricular arrhythmia.**
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**  
  – Anoxic brain injury (global ischaemia) – main cause of death/morbidity post-ROSC.  
  – Myocardial stunning (transient LV dysfunction) – treat with inotropes if required.  
  – Rib fractures, pneumothorax, liver/spleen laceration from CPR.  
  – Recurrent VF/VT within 24 hours.  
  – Aspiration pneumonia (consider antibiotics and lung physiotherapy).  
- **Chronic:**  
  – Permanent neurological deficits (cognitive, motor).  
  – ICD-related complications (infection, lead displacement, inappropriate shocks).  
  – Psychological sequelae: PTSD, anxiety, depression.
sections.Prognosis:
- Overall survival to hospital discharge after VF arrest: 15–30% (higher if witnessed, bystander CPR, early defibrillation).  
- Good neurological outcome at 6 months in ~50% of survivors. Favourable predictors: initial shockable rhythm, time to defibrillation <4 minutes, GCS >5 at 72h post-ROSC.
sections.Pearls (actions to do):
- **Use defibrillator pads for rhythm check before charging** – faster than attaching ECG leads and allows immediate shock.  
- **Ensure good CPR while defibrillator charges** – reduce no-flow time. After shock, resume CPR immediately for 2 minutes without pausing to check pulse.  
- **After ROSC, do a 12-lead ECG** – look for ST-elevation or ischaemic changes to trigger emergency angiography.  
- **Document arrest times meticulously** (collapse, CPR start, defibrillation, drugs) – needed for cardiac arrest audit.
sections.Pitfalls (errors to avoid):
- **Do not give drugs before defibrillation** – defibrillation is the priority for shockable rhythms.  
- **Do not pause CPR for >10 seconds for rhythm check** – use the 2-minute cycle (Resus Council UK) for rhythm checks.  
- **Do not use paddle placement incorrectly** – anterior-lateral correct placement: one pad below right clavicle, one pad at left mid-axilla (avoid breast tissue, ensure pad-to-skin contact, no gel overlap).  
- **Do not leave the defibrillator unattended** – ensure charged and ready for next shock.
sections.Safety Netting (if patient goes home – e:
- **Return if:**  
  – Recurrent syncope or palpitations  
  – ICD shock (especially if more than one shock in 24 hours)  
  – Chest pain or new dyspnoea  
  – Any new neurological symptom (weakness, confusion)
- **Follow-up:**  
  – Cardiology clinic within 4 weeks  
  – ICD interrogation at 1 month and then every 3–6 months
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Immediately call cardiac arrest team (2222)**
- **Post-ROSC escalation to ICU** if any of:  
  – GCS <9 (or patient not following commands)  
  – Mean arterial pressure <65 mmHg despite fluids  
  – Lactate >4 mmol/L (persistent)  
  – High vasopressor requirement  
  – Recurrent VF/VT despite first-line drugs  
  – NEWS2 ≥7  
- **Call senior clinician (Reg/Consultant) if:**  
  – VF refractory to 3 shocks + amiodarone  
  – Uncertain cause (consider metabolic/toxic)  
  – Patient is pregnant (modify resuscitation: manual uterine displacement)  
  – Family/situation requires immediate complex decision-making (e.g., DNACPR, withdrawal of care)

rawMarkdown:
## Ventricular Fibrillation (VF)

**One-liner:** Chaotic, pulseless ventricular rhythm causing cardiac arrest; immediate defibrillation is the only effective treatment—time is brain and heart.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Pulseless Ventricular Tachycardia (VT):** Treat identically to VF—shock immediately. Single finding: ECG showing regular broad-complex tachycardia with loss of cardiac output; defibrillator perceives a shockable rhythm.
- **Asystole / PEA:** Shock may waste time. Single finding: defibrillator shows a non-shockable rhythm (flatline or organised complexes but no pulse) → switch to CPR, adrenaline, and reversible cause search immediately.
- **Tension pneumothorax causing PEA:** Look for tracheal deviation, absent breath sounds, distended neck veins, hypotension. Immediate needle decompression before any other intervention.

### Presentation
- Sudden collapse, unresponsive, no normal breathing (agonal gasps may occur in first minutes)
- No palpable carotid or femoral pulse
- Seizure-like activity (global cerebral hypoxia) can be confused with epilepsy
- Skin colour: pale, grey, then cyanotic
- ECG / defibrillator monitor: chaotic, irregular ventricular waveform, no identifiable QRS complexes

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Most likely:** Pulseless VT → same rhythm on monitor but wider, more organised complexes; treat with immediate defibrillation. **Splitter:** Defibrillator rhythm check: shockable vs. non-shockable. VF/VT require defibrillation; asystole/PEA do not.
- **Less likely:** Asystole (flatline, no electrical activity); PEA (organised rhythm but no output); fine VF may mimic asystole on ECG – check gain and look for any electrical activity.

### Investigations
- **Bedside (first hour):**  
  – Attach defibrillator pads immediately for rhythm analysis (do not delay defibrillation)  
  – Check pulse and breathing before rhythm check? No – in witnessed arrest with monitor showing shockable rhythm, defibrillate first.  
  – After ROSC: 12-lead ECG, point-of-care ultrasound (if skilled) for LV function, tamponade, pneumothorax.
- **Bloods (after ROSC):**  
  – Arterial blood gas: pH, pCO2, base deficit, lactate, glucose, K+, Ca2+  
  – FBC, U&E, Mg2+, troponin (high-sensitivity), coagulation screen, D-dimer (if PE suspected)  
  – Consider toxicology screen if suspected overdose.
- **Imaging (after ROSC, stable):**  
  – CXR: endotracheal tube placement, tension pneumothorax, aspiration  
  – CT head: if suspected intracranial cause  
  – CT pulmonary angiography: if PE suspected (high D-dimer, low PaO2/FiO2)
- **Special tests:**  
  – Echocardiogram within 24h to assess LV function and structural cause  
  – Coronary angiography urgently if ST-elevation on post-ROSC ECG (or high suspicion of ACS)
- **Never-miss test:**  
  – **Immediate rhythm check via defibrillator pads** – do not waste time placing leads; treat based on defibrillator rhythm. Do not delay defibrillation for any other test.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**  
  – Call cardiac arrest team (2222) – or pull emergency bell.  
  – Start high-quality CPR immediately (30:2) while defibrillator is prepared.  
  – Defibrillate: biphasic 150–200 J (monophasic 360 J). After shock, resume CPR immediately for 2 minutes without checking pulse/rhythm.  
  – If still in VF/VT: defibrillate again, continue CPR cycles.  
  – After 3 shocks: give adrenaline 1mg IV and amiodarone 300mg IV after the 3rd shock (then 150mg after 5th shock if refractory).  
  – Follow Resuscitation Council UK ALS algorithm: shockable rhythm pathway.  
  – Treat reversible causes: 4Hs + 4Ts (hypoxia, hypovolaemia, hypo-/hyperkalaemia, hypothermia; thrombosis [MI/PE], tension pneumothorax, tamponade, toxins).
- **Ward (first 24–72h) – if ROSC achieved:**  
  – Targeted temperature management (32–36°C) for 24h if unconscious – use cooling blanket, ice packs, cold IV saline (check local protocol).  
  – Transfer to ICU/cardiac high-dependency for post-cardiac arrest syndrome management.  
  – Urgent echocardiogram and cardiology review for ICD consideration.  
  – Treat underlying cause: if STEMI → primary PCI; if electrolyte disturbance → correct (K+ 4.5–5.0, Mg2+ 0.8–1.2 mmol/L).  
  – Neurological assessment: pupillary response, corneal reflexes, motor responses. Discuss neuroprognostication with senior.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**  
  – ICD implantation for all survivors of VF not due to reversible cause.  
  – Genetic counselling if channelopathy suspected (e.g., Brugada, long QT).  
  – Cardiology follow-up at 6–8 weeks, then annually.  
  – Consider cardiac MRI if myocarditis or structural disease possible.
- **Default orders (if admitted post-ROSC):**  
  – Fluids: balanced crystalloid (e.g., Plasmalyte) 30 mL/kg loading if hypotensive; maintenance at 1 mL/kg/h (adjust for renal function)  
  – VTE prophylaxis: subcutaneous low molecular weight heparin when no active bleeding (e.g., enoxaparin 40mg daily, but hold for 24h if thrombolysis given)  
  – Diet: NBM until safe swallow assessed (GCS <12 → keep NBM for airway protection)  
  – Observations: continuous ECG monitoring, hourly neuro status (GCS, pupil size), BP, SpO2, capnography if intubated. Level 2 care (HDU) minimum.

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents:**  
  – Adrenaline 1mg (1:10,000) IV every 3–5 minutes during cardiac arrest.  
  – Amiodarone 300mg IV (over 5 minutes) after 3rd shock for refractory VF/VT; second dose 150mg IV after 5th shock.  
- **Common alternatives:**  
  – Lidocaine 1–1.5 mg/kg IV (only if amiodarone unavailable or contraindicated) – not first line.  
  – Magnesium sulfate 2g IV (over 1–2 minutes) for polymorphic VT/torsade de pointes (especially if low Mg2+).  
  – Sodium bicarbonate 50 mmol IV only if severe acidosis (pH <7.1) or tricyclic overdose – [check local policy].  
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**  
  – Amiodarone: caution with other QTc-prolonging drugs (e.g., macrolides, ciprofloxacin, antipsychotics).  
  – Adrenaline: may increase myocardial O2 demand; avoid in hypothermic arrest until core >30°C.  
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**  
  – No major dose adjustment for amiodarone or adrenaline in renal impairment.  
  – Amiodarone hepatic excretion – avoid in severe liver disease if possible, or reduce dose (discuss with pharmacist).  
- **Most dangerous dose error:**  
  – **Do not give IV amiodarone as a push in a conscious patient (risk of hypotension/bradycardia).** In cardiac arrest, push slowly over 5 minutes only if directed by ALS algorithm.  
  – **Adrenaline: NEVER give as rapid IV bolus in a patient with pulse – risk of malignant ventricular arrhythmia.**

### Complications
- **Acute:**  
  – Anoxic brain injury (global ischaemia) – main cause of death/morbidity post-ROSC.  
  – Myocardial stunning (transient LV dysfunction) – treat with inotropes if required.  
  – Rib fractures, pneumothorax, liver/spleen laceration from CPR.  
  – Recurrent VF/VT within 24 hours.  
  – Aspiration pneumonia (consider antibiotics and lung physiotherapy).  
- **Chronic:**  
  – Permanent neurological deficits (cognitive, motor).  
  – ICD-related complications (infection, lead displacement, inappropriate shocks).  
  – Psychological sequelae: PTSD, anxiety, depression.

### Prognosis
- Overall survival to hospital discharge after VF arrest: 15–30% (higher if witnessed, bystander CPR, early defibrillation).  
- Good neurological outcome at 6 months in ~50% of survivors. Favourable predictors: initial shockable rhythm, time to defibrillation <4 minutes, GCS >5 at 72h post-ROSC.

### Pearls (actions to do)
- **Use defibrillator pads for rhythm check before charging** – faster than attaching ECG leads and allows immediate shock.  
- **Ensure good CPR while defibrillator charges** – reduce no-flow time. After shock, resume CPR immediately for 2 minutes without pausing to check pulse.  
- **After ROSC, do a 12-lead ECG** – look for ST-elevation or ischaemic changes to trigger emergency angiography.  
- **Document arrest times meticulously** (collapse, CPR start, defibrillation, drugs) – needed for cardiac arrest audit.

### Pitfalls (errors to avoid)
- **Do not give drugs before defibrillation** – defibrillation is the priority for shockable rhythms.  
- **Do not pause CPR for >10 seconds for rhythm check** – use the 2-minute cycle (Resus Council UK) for rhythm checks.  
- **Do not use paddle placement incorrectly** – anterior-lateral correct placement: one pad below right clavicle, one pad at left mid-axilla (avoid breast tissue, ensure pad-to-skin contact, no gel overlap).  
- **Do not leave the defibrillator unattended** – ensure charged and ready for next shock.

### Safety Netting (if patient goes home – extremely rare after VF; applies after ICD implantation/discharge)
- **Return if:**  
  – Recurrent syncope or palpitations  
  – ICD shock (especially if more than one shock in 24 hours)  
  – Chest pain or new dyspnoea  
  – Any new neurological symptom (weakness, confusion)
- **Follow-up:**  
  – Cardiology clinic within 4 weeks  
  – ICD interrogation at 1 month and then every 3–6 months

### Escalation Criteria
- **Immediately call cardiac arrest team (2222)**
- **Post-ROSC escalation to ICU** if any of:  
  – GCS <9 (or patient not following commands)  
  – Mean arterial pressure <65 mmHg despite fluids  
  – Lactate >4 mmol/L (persistent)  
  – High vasopressor requirement  
  – Recurrent VF/VT despite first-line drugs  
  – NEWS2 ≥7  
- **Call senior clinician (Reg/Consultant) if:**  
  – VF refractory to 3 shocks + amiodarone  
  – Uncertain cause (consider metabolic/toxic)  
  – Patient is pregnant (modify resuscitation: manual uterine displacement)  
  – Family/situation requires immediate complex decision-making (e.g., DNACPR, withdrawal of care)
